lessons.title: Traditional I/O
lessons.summary: Learn the hierarchical structure of byte streams centered around Java's InputStream/OutputStream. Understand how to use FileInputStream/FileOutputStream and BufferedReader/BufferedWriter.

contents.title: Java I/O Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basics of [[InputStream]] and [[OutputStream]] (byte streams)
- How to use FileInputStream / FileOutputStream
- How to improve I/O performance with BufferedStream

You will learn the fundamentals of reading and writing files.

contents.title: InputStream / OutputStream Hierarchy
contents.body:
InputStream is the abstract base [[class]] for byte input, centered around the read() [[method]].
OutputStream is the abstract base class for byte output, centered around the write() method.
Key subclasses include FileInputStream, FileOutputStream, ByteArrayInputStream, and ByteArrayOutputStream.
BufferedInputStream / BufferedOutputStream improve I/O efficiency through buffering.
DataInputStream / DataOutputStream provide reading and writing of [[primitive types]].
ObjectInputStream / ObjectOutputStream are used for object serialization and deserialization.
contents.note: Remember that InputStream/OutputStream work at the byte level, while Reader/Writer work at the character level.

contents.title: Using FileInputStream / FileOutputStream
contents.body:
FileInputStream is a stream that reads bytes from a file.
FileOutputStream is a stream that writes bytes to a file.
You pass a file path or File object to the [[constructor]].
read() reads one byte and returns -1 at end of file.
You need to release resources with close(). Using [[try-with-resources]] is recommended.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: BufferedReader / BufferedWriter
contents.body:
[[BufferedReader]] adds buffering to a character input stream.
The readLine() method reads text one line at a time.
BufferedWriter adds buffering to a character output stream.
The newLine() method writes a platform-dependent line separator.
You can convert a byte stream to a character stream via InputStreamReader.
contents.note: **BufferedReader**.readLine() returns null when end of file is reached. An empty line returns an empty string ("").

contents.title: Exam Prep: Traditional I/O
contents.body:
Clearly distinguish between byte streams (InputStream/OutputStream) and character streams (Reader/Writer).
The decorator pattern (wrapping **streams**) combinations are tested on the exam.
Resource leaks from forgetting to call close() are an important topic.
Using try-with-resources automatically closes [[AutoCloseable]] resources.
Remember the conditions under which read() returns -1 and readLine() returns null.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the InputStream class is correct?
quizzes.explanation: InputStream is an abstract class that reads data byte by byte. The read() method reads one byte and returns -1 when the end of the stream is reached. Character-level reading is handled by the Reader class.

choices.text: It is an abstract class that reads data byte by byte
It is a concrete class that reads data character by character
read() returns 0 at the end of the stream
There is no need to call close()

quizzes.question: BufferedReader's readLine() method returns ___ when the end of the file is reached.
quizzes.explanation: BufferedReader.readLine() returns null when the end of the file is reached. For empty lines, it returns an empty string (""). The read() method returns -1 when reading bytes/characters one at a time.

quizzes.question: Which class adds buffering to a byte stream?
quizzes.explanation: BufferedInputStream is a class that wraps an InputStream to add buffering. BufferedReader is a buffering class for character streams (Reader). DataInputStream is for reading primitive types.

lessons.title: Reader and Writer
lessons.summary: Learn character stream operations with FileReader/FileWriter, character encoding specification with Charset, and safe resource management with try-with-resources.

contents.title: Character Stream Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The basics of Reader and Writer (character streams)
- FileReader / FileWriter, **BufferedReader** / **BufferedWriter**
- Differences from byte streams (character encoding support)

Character streams are suited for text file processing.

contents.title: Using FileReader / FileWriter
contents.body:
FileWriter is a [[class]] that writes character data to a file.
Specifying true as the second argument in the [[constructor]] enables append mode.
FileReader is a class that reads character data from a file.
The read() [[method]] reads one character at a time and returns -1 at end of file.
You can also read into a char array in bulk with read(char[]).
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Character Encoding (Charset)
contents.body:
The [[Charset]] class represents a character encoding.
The StandardCharsets class defines constants such as UTF_8, ISO_8859_1, and US_ASCII.
You can also obtain a Charset from a string with Charset.forName("UTF-8").
If no encoding is specified, the system's default encoding is used.
Reading and writing with different encodings will cause garbled text.
Using InputStreamReader, you can convert a byte stream to a character stream with a specific encoding.
contents.note: Starting with Java 18, the default encoding is UTF-8. In earlier versions, it was platform-dependent.

contents.title: try-with-resources and I/O
contents.body:
The [[try-with-resources]] statement automatically calls close() on resources that implement [[AutoCloseable]].
Since all I/O classes implement AutoCloseable, they should be used with try-with-resources.
Multiple resources can be declared separated by semicolons.
Resources are closed in the reverse order of declaration (last in, first out).
Since Java 9, previously declared final or effectively final [[variables]] can be used in try-with-resources.
contents.note: If an [[exception]] and a close() exception occur simultaneously in try-with-resources, the close() exception becomes a suppressed exception.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Reader and Writer
contents.body:
The ability to specify a Charset in FileReader and FileWriter constructors is available from Java 11 onward.
InputStreamReader is a bridge for converting byte streams to character streams.
The resource close order in try-with-resources (reverse of declaration order) is tested on the exam.
Since Java 9, using effectively final variables directly in try-with-resources is also important.
Calling flush() after write(), or calling close(), ensures the buffer's contents are written to the file.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes the role of InputStreamReader?
quizzes.explanation: InputStreamReader is a bridge class that converts a byte stream (InputStream) to a character stream (Reader). By specifying a Charset in the constructor, you can convert bytes to characters using a specific encoding.

choices.text: Converts a byte stream to a character stream
Converts a character stream to a byte stream
Reads a file byte by byte

quizzes.question: What is the output when the following code is executed?
quizzes.explanation: StringWriter is a Writer that writes to an in-memory string buffer. write("Hello") writes "Hello", write(' ') writes a space, and write("World") writes "World". toString() returns the concatenated string "Hello World".

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. Which part is problematic?
quizzes.explanation: Variables used in try-with-resources cannot be reassigned within the try block. Since Java 9, pre-declared variables can be used in try-with-resources, but those variables must be final or effectively final. Reassigning fr inside the try block causes a compilation error.

choices.text: The variable fr used in try-with-resources is being reassigned
The return type of fr.read() is invalid

quizzes.question: Complete the code that reads a file using NIO's Path and Files.
quizzes.explanation: Path.get() obtains a file path, and Files.readAllLines() reads all lines. Since Java 11, Path.of() can also be used.

lessons.title: NIO.2 Path
lessons.summary: Learn the NIO.2 Path interface. Understand creating paths with Paths.get() and Path.of(), and path manipulation methods such as resolve(), relativize(), and normalize().

contents.title: What Is NIO.2?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The **NIO**.2 Path interface (Java 7+)
- Creating paths with Paths.get() and Path.of()
- Operations such as resolve(), relativize(), and normalize()

This is the modern file path API that replaces the traditional java.io.File.

contents.title: Creating a Path
contents.body:
The Path interface is located in java.nio.file.Path.
You can create a Path object with the Paths.get() [[method]] (Java 7+).
You can also create a Path with the Path.of() method (Java 11+).
Paths can be specified with multiple arguments for path elements.
You can convert a File object to a Path with toPath(), and a Path to a File with toFile().
Both relative and absolute paths are supported.
contents.note: Paths.get() and Path.of() are functionally equivalent. Path.of() is recommended from Java 11 onward.

contents.title: resolve / relativize / normalize
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| resolve() | Joins paths. If the argument is an absolute path, the argument is returned as-is. |
| relativize() | Computes the relative path between two paths. Both must be either relative or absolute. |
| normalize() | Resolves redundant elements such as . and .. |
These methods return a Path object and do not modify the original Path ([[immutable object]]).
contents.note: Mixing absolute and relative paths in relativize() throws an IllegalArgumentException.

contents.title: Path Information Methods
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| getFileName() | Returns the last element of the path (file name). |
| getParent() | Returns the parent directory path. Returns null for root-only paths. |
| getRoot() | Returns the root element. Returns null for relative paths. |
| getNameCount() | Returns the number of name elements in the path (excluding root). |
| getName(int) | Returns the name element at the specified index. |
| toAbsolutePath() | Converts to an absolute path. |

contents.title: Exam Prep: Path Methods
contents.body:
Passing an absolute path to resolve() causes the argument to be returned as-is — this is a frequently tested point.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| relativize() | Throws an exception if the paths are not both absolute or both relative. |
| normalize() | Processes only the path string without accessing the file system. |
| getNameCount() | Does not include the root element. getName(0) is the element directly under the root. |
subpath(begin, end) uses a range from begin (inclusive) to end (exclusive), zero-based.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: resolve() joins the argument to the original path when the argument is a relative path. It concatenates file.txt to /home/user/docs, resulting in /home/user/docs/file.txt.

quizzes.question: What is the return value of Path.of("/a/b/c").getNameCount()?
quizzes.explanation: getNameCount() returns the number of name elements in the path. The root element (/) is not included in the count. The name elements of /a/b/c are a, b, and c — three elements.

quizzes.question: The normalize() method of Path resolves redundant ___ and .. in the path and returns the normalized path.
quizzes.explanation: The normalize() method resolves redundant elements in the path string — . (current directory) and .. (parent directory) — and returns the normalized path. It does not access the file system.

quizzes.question: Select all valid methods of the NIO.2 (java.nio.file) Files class.
quizzes.explanation: The Files class provides readAllLines(), copy(), exists(), walk(), and more. open() is not a method of Files — use newInputStream() and similar methods instead.

lessons.title: NIO.2 Files
lessons.summary: Learn file I/O operations with the Files class. Understand file reading and writing with readAllLines() and write(), and file manipulation methods such as copy(), move(), delete(), and exists().

contents.title: Overview of the Files Class
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Simple file I/O with Files.readAllLines() and Files.write()
- Files.copy(), Files.move(), Files.delete()
- Retrieving file attributes (size, lastModifiedTime, etc.)

With the **NIO**.2 Files class, many file operations can be written in a single line.

contents.title: Reading and Writing Files
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| Files.readAllLines() | Reads all lines as a List<String> |
| Files.readString() | Reads the content as a String (Java 11+) |
| Files.write() | Writes a byte array or a list of strings to a file |
| Files.writeString() | Writes a string to a file (Java 11+) |
You can specify the write mode (overwrite, append, etc.) with OpenOption.
contents.note: readAllLines() loads the entire file into memory, so for large files, use Files.lines() instead.

contents.title: Copying, Moving, and Deleting Files
contents.body:
Files.copy() copies a file. By default, a FileAlreadyExistsException is thrown if the target file already exists.
Files.move() moves or renames a file.
CopyOption lets you specify overwriting or atomic operations.
Files.delete() deletes a file and throws a NoSuchFileException if the file does not exist.
Files.deleteIfExists() deletes the file only if it exists, returning false instead of throwing an [[exception]] if it does not.
contents.note: If REPLACE_EXISTING is not specified in copy/move and the target file already exists, a FileAlreadyExistsException is thrown.

contents.title: Checking File Information
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| Files.exists() | Checks whether a file or directory exists |
| Files.isDirectory() | Checks whether the target is a directory |
| Files.isRegularFile() | Checks whether the target is a regular file |
| Files.size() | Returns the file size in bytes |
| Files.getLastModifiedTime() | Returns the last modified time |
You can check access permissions with Files.isReadable(), Files.isWritable(), and Files.isExecutable().

contents.title: Exam Prep: Files Class
contents.body:
Remember the default behavior of Files.copy() and Files.move() (no overwrite).
The difference in behavior between delete() and deleteIfExists() is tested on the exam.
readAllLines() returns a List<String>, while readString() returns a String (Java 11+).
The difference between createDirectory() and createDirectories() is also important (whether intermediate directories are automatically created).
Memorize the key constants in StandardCopyOption and StandardOpenOption.

quizzes.question: Which option is required to overwrite an existing file when using Files.copy()?
quizzes.explanation: By default, Files.copy() throws a FileAlreadyExistsException if the target file already exists. To overwrite, you need to specify StandardCopyOption.REPLACE_EXISTING.

quizzes.question: Files.___() returns false if the file does not exist, while Files.delete() throws a ___.
quizzes.explanation: Files.deleteIfExists() returns false if the file does not exist, without throwing an exception. On the other hand, Files.delete() throws a NoSuchFileException if the file does not exist.

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. Which part is problematic?
quizzes.explanation: Files.createDirectory() creates only a single directory level. If intermediate directories (myDir) do not exist in a path like myDir/subDir, a NoSuchFileException is thrown. To create intermediate directories as well, use Files.createDirectories().

choices.text: createDirectory() does not auto-create intermediate dirs; createDirectories() should be used
The path in writeString() is invalid
Slashes cannot be included in Path.of() arguments
The resolve() method name is wrong

lessons.title: Directory Operations and Streams
lessons.summary: Learn NIO.2 directory operation methods (Files.list, Files.walk, Files.find), DirectoryStream, and integration with the Stream API using Files.lines().

contents.title: Directory Operations and Streams Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Obtaining a Stream of directories with Files.list() and Files.walk()
- Searching for files matching criteria with Files.find()
- Differences from DirectoryStream

Efficient file operations combining the [[Stream API]] with NIO.2.

contents.title: Files.list() and Files.walk()
contents.body:
Files.list() returns the entries (files and subdirectories) directly under a directory as a Stream<Path>.
It does not traverse recursively — depth 1 only.
Files.walk() recursively traverses the directory tree in depth-first order.
You can specify maxDepth (maximum depth) as the second argument.
Both return Stream<Path>, so they must be closed with close() or [[try-with-resources]] after use.
contents.note: The Streams returned by Files.list() and Files.walk() hold I/O resources, so they must be closed.

contents.title: Files.find() and DirectoryStream
contents.body:
Files.find() recursively searches for files and specifies conditions with a BiPredicate<Path, BasicFileAttributes>.
DirectoryStream is an [[interface]] for iterating over the contents of a directory.
You can obtain a DirectoryStream with Files.newDirectoryStream() and filter with glob patterns.
Since DirectoryStream implements Iterable, it can be used with an [[enhanced for loop]].
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Files.lines() and the Stream API
contents.body:
Files.lines() lazily reads each line of a file as a Stream<String>.
Unlike readAllLines(), it does not hold all lines in memory, making it suitable for large files.
It can be combined with various Stream API operations (filter, map, collect, etc.).
The returned Stream also holds I/O resources, so close() is required.
You can specify a [[Charset]] as the second argument; the default is UTF-8.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Directory Operations and Streams
contents.body:
Clearly remember that Files.list() covers only immediate entries, while Files.walk() is recursive.
The default maxDepth for Files.walk() is Integer.MAX_VALUE.
All Streams returned by Files.list(), Files.walk(), Files.find(), and Files.lines() must be closed.
DirectoryStream is not a Stream but an Iterable, and is used with an enhanced [[for loop]].
The BiPredicate in Files.find() accepts (Path, BasicFileAttributes).

quizzes.question: If the src directory directly contains 2 files (App.java and Main.java) and 1 subdirectory (utils), how many lines would be output by this code?
quizzes.explanation: Files.list() returns all entries (files and subdirectories) directly under the directory. Since there are 3 entries — App.java, Main.java, and utils — count() returns 3.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between Files.walk() and Files.list()?
quizzes.explanation: Files.walk() recursively traverses the directory tree and returns all files including those in subdirectories as a Stream<Path>. Files.list() returns only entries directly under the directory and does not traverse recursively.

choices.text: walk() traverses recursively, while list() returns only direct entries
list() traverses recursively, while walk() returns only direct entries
walk() returns List<Path>, while list() returns Stream<Path>
Both behave the same way and only differ in name

quizzes.question: Which statement about Files.lines() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Files.lines() lazily reads each line and returns it as a Stream<String>. Unlike Files.readAllLines(), it does not load all lines into memory at once, making it suitable for large files. The returned Stream holds I/O resources, so close() is required.

choices.text: Lazily reads each line of the file and returns it as a Stream<String>
Immediately returns all lines of the file as a List<String>
The returned Stream does not need to be closed
Returns each line of a binary file as Stream<byte[]>
